name: codebase-cleanup-tech-debt
description: You are a technical debt expert specializing in identifying, quantifying, and prioritizing technical debt in software projects. Analyze the codebase to uncover debt, assess its impact, and create acti
capabilities: Conduct comprehensive technical debt inventories across code, architecture, testing, documentation, and infrastructure categories
Calculate ROI and cost impact for each debt item using developer hourly rates and time savings
Create prioritized remediation roadmaps with quick wins, medium-term improvements, and long-term initiatives
Design automated quality gates and debt budgets to prevent accumulation of new debt
Generate stakeholder-ready reports with metrics, risks, and executive summaries
author: sickn33
securitySummary: All 30 static findings evaluated as false positives. The skill is purely instructional content for technical debt analysis. Dollar amounts in examples ($150/hour) were misidentified as shell commands. Generic technical terms were misidentified as cryptographic algorithms. Prevention planning was misidentified as system reconnaissance. No actual security risks present.
valueStatement: This skill helps development teams identify, quantify, and prioritize technical debt in their codebase. It provides structured methodologies for assessing debt impact, calculating ROI for remediation efforts, and creating actionable plans to improve code quality over time.
limitations: Does not execute code changes - provides guidance only
Does not access external systems or perform network requests
Requires human judgment for final prioritization decisions
Metrics and calculations require manual verification for accuracy

useCases.targetUser: Tech leads and engineering managers seeking visibility into codebase health
useCases.title: Initial Debt Assessment
useCases.description: Run a comprehensive scan of an existing codebase to document all technical debt, categorize by type, and prioritize based on business impact.

useCases.targetUser: Development teams allocating time for debt reduction in sprints
useCases.title: Sprint Planning Support
useCases.description: Identify quick wins and high-ROI refactoring tasks to include in upcoming sprint planning sessions.

useCases.targetUser: Engineering directors and VPs making budget decisions
useCases.title: Stakeholder Communication
useCases.description: Generate executive summaries and ROI projections to justify technical debt remediation investments to business leadership.

promptTemplates.title: Quick Debt Scan
promptTemplates.prompt: Analyze my codebase for technical debt. Focus on finding: duplicated code patterns, high complexity functions, missing tests, and outdated dependencies. List the top 10 debt items with estimated remediation effort.
promptTemplates.scenario: Beginner user wanting a quick overview of debt hotspots

promptTemplates.title: Comprehensive Debt Audit
promptTemplates.prompt: Conduct a full technical debt audit of our codebase. Include: code debt (duplication, complexity, structure), architecture debt (design flaws, tech stack), testing debt (coverage, quality), documentation debt, and infrastructure debt. For each category, quantify the impact and create a prioritized remediation plan.

promptTemplates.title: ROI-Focused Prioritization
promptTemplates.prompt: Calculate the ROI for our top 20 technical debt items. For each, estimate: developer time lost per month, bug rate impact, and onboarding delay. Rank by highest ROI opportunity and create a quarterly remediation roadmap.

promptTemplates.title: Debt Prevention Strategy
promptTemplates.prompt: Design a debt prevention strategy for our team. Include: pre-commit hooks for code quality, CI pipeline gates, code review requirements, and a debt budget policy. Explain how to track compliance and measure success.
promptTemplates.scenario: Establishing processes to prevent future debt accumulation

bestPractices: Start with quick wins that show immediate ROI to build team buy-in for larger refactoring efforts
Always quantify debt impact in developer hours and currency to communicate with stakeholders effectively
Use feature flags for gradual refactoring to reduce risk and enable incremental value delivery
antiPatterns: Attempting to fix all debt at once - prioritize based on impact and effort for sustainable progress
Ignoring testing debt - low coverage compounds all other debt types and slows future changes
Not tracking debt over time - without metrics, teams cannot measure improvement or regression

faq.question: What types of technical debt does this skill analyze?
faq.answer: The skill analyzes five main categories: code debt (duplication, complexity, structure), architecture debt (design flaws, technology stack), testing debt (coverage gaps, test quality), documentation debt (missing docs, outdated guides), and infrastructure debt (deployment issues, monitoring gaps).

faq.question: Does this skill make changes to my codebase?
faq.answer: No. This skill provides analysis, recommendations, and guidance. It does not execute any code changes. All refactoring work must be performed manually or with other tools.

faq.question: How does the skill calculate ROI for debt remediation?
faq.answer: ROI is calculated by estimating the time saved per month from fixing the debt, multiplied by the developer hourly rate, compared to the effort required to implement the fix. Default rate is $150/hour but can be adjusted.

faq.question: Can this skill work with any programming language?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill provides a framework for analyzing debt that is language-agnostic. The specific metrics and tools mentioned can be adapted to any technology stack.

faq.question: How often should we run technical debt analysis?
faq.answer: Teams should conduct comprehensive analysis quarterly, with monthly quick scans to track progress. After major releases or significant refactoring, additional analysis helps identify new debt accumulation.

faq.question: What is a debt budget and how do we implement one?
faq.answer: A debt budget limits how much new debt can be introduced per quarter (typically 2-5%). It requires teams to fix existing debt before adding new debt. Implementation uses automated tools to track complexity, duplication, and coverage metrics.
